original_hypothesis.title: Immutable Domain Separator Enables Cross-Chain Signature Replay After Chain Forks
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The Signer contract computes _DOMAIN_SEPARATOR once at deployment using block.chainid and stores it as immutable. _CHAIN_ID is stored but never checked at runtime in _recoverSignerFromPayload. After a chain fork where block.chainid changes on one fork, valid rental creation signatures become replayable on both chains because the same domain separator is used, despite the chain IDs diverging.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Standard EIP-712 implementations (e.g., OpenZeppelin) recompute the domain separator if block.chainid differs from the stored chain ID. The Signer contract stores _CHAIN_ID but never compares it to block.chainid before using _DOMAIN_SEPARATOR. After a fork, signatures intended for one chain work on both.
preconditions: A chain fork occurs where block.chainid changes on the forked chain
Valid protocol signer signatures exist from before the fork
Both chains have the same protocol deployment

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Same signature validates on both chains after fork
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Post-fork, for all existing valid signatures

operational_questions: Is a chain fork realistically expected for the target chains?
Could signature expiration mitigate replay (how long are signatures valid)?
Would replayed rentals on the forked chain cause actual harm?
validation_requirements: Verify _CHAIN_ID is never compared to block.chainid in any code path
Test signature validity after simulated chain fork
Assess if existing signature expiration limits the replay window

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Collect valid rental creation signatures from the original chain
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for or trigger a chain fork
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Replay collected signatures on the forked chain
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Unauthorized rentals created on the forked chain using replayed signatures
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires a chain fork event (rare, out of attacker's control)

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Protocol signer can be rotated; requires redeployment on forked chain
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All rental creation on the forked chain

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Duplicate rental creation signatures on different chain IDs

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
